data.title: Blue Bird Corporation: A Strong Play, Even In Light Of Tariff Terror
data.description:
One company that I have been very bullish on lately he has been electric bus producer Blue Bird Corporation (NASDAQ:BLBD). Even though I am cautious about the electric vehicle market at this time, management has proven itself to be good operators for the business. This was proven yet again on May 7th when management announced financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Even though earnings per share fell short of analysts’ expectations, revenue and adjusted earnings per share came in higher than what analysts were anticipating. Management narrowed guidance for 2025 and reaffirmed the long term outlook for the business. When you add on top of this the fact that the stock is still cheap, despite rising 7.6% since my previous article on it in February, and I believe that keeping the business rated a ‘strong buy’ is appropriate.

Management keeps doing well

On May 7th, the management team at Blue Bird Corporation announced financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue totaled $358.9 million. This translated to an increase of 3.8% compared to the $345.9 million the business reported one year earlier. The revenue reported by the company ended up being $10.6 million greater than what analysts were hoping to see. During this window of time, the company benefited from an increase in the number of units delivered from 2,254 last year to 2,295 this year.

A key growth area for the company would be its electric powered buses. During the quarter, the firm delivered 265 of these. Management did not reveal how many they delivered the previous year. But we do know that total alternative power buses, which include gasoline ones, propane ones, compressed natural gas ones, and electric ones, resulted in revenue of $199.5 million compared to the $180.2 million reported the previous year. By comparison, diesel bus revenue for the business dropped from $123.4 million to $117.6 million. I would have liked to have seen parts related revenue grow as well. But it ended up falling from $27.3 million to $25.5 million.

In terms of profitability, the picture was mixed, but generally positive. Earnings per share remained flat year over year at $0.79. And, unfortunately, this missed analysts’ expectations by $0.16. A rise in share-based compensation related mostly to the retirement of its former President and CEO, as well as higher labor costs elsewhere, caused selling, general, and administrative costs, to grow by $9.6 million. Naturally, this meant that net income remained flat at $26 million. Fortunately, adjusted earnings per share ended up rising from $0.89 last year to $0.96 this year, taking net profits for the company up from $29.2 million to $31.5 million. The good news is that the adjusted earnings per share reported by the company ended up being $0.01 greater than what analysts were hoping to see.

We should also pay attention to other profitability metrics as well. Operating cash flow did plummet from $54.6 million to $27.8 million. However, if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $31.1 million to $34.4 million. In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Once again, the company benefited from a rise in the number of deliveries from 4,383 to 4,425, which pushed revenue up from $663.6 million to $672.7 million. We did see declines in operating cash flow and adjusted operating cash flow. But outside of this, the bottom line for the business improved across the board.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year, management has narrowed guidance. The firm used to think that revenue would be between $1.40 billion and $1.50 billion. That range has now been reduced to between $1.425 billion and $1.475 billion. The company did the same thing regarding EBITDA. They now expect the range to be between $190 million and $210 million. Previous guidance called for this to range between $185 million and $215 million. If management does end up hitting the midpoint of guidance for the year, it would likely mean adjusted operating cash flow of around $135.2 million. All of this should be achieved with around 9,300 deliveries, which would be up from the previous guidance of 9,250.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These are multiples that I would typically associate with an undervalued business. What's really great, however, is that management thinks we are only getting started with the growth for the business. In the image below, you can see that the company is targeting around $1.60 billion in revenue in the not-too-distant future. This will be either next year or the year after. And it would come from a rise in the number of deliveries to 10,000. We don't know how many of these will be electrified vehicles. But we do know that, from 2024 to 2025, the company expects that the number of electric buses delivered will have grown from 704 to 900.

By 2028 and beyond, the company is targeting between 11,000 and over 12,000 vehicle deliveries each year. This would push revenue at that time up to between $1.85 billion and $2 billion. At the same time, the company is forecasting continued margin improvement. This year, they expect an EBITDA margin of around 14%. That's up from the 13.6% seen last year. And eventually, the company sees this expanding to between 14.5% and 15%.

One really exciting thing is that management has provided additional details regarding what the future of the North American school bus industry should look like. The bad news here is that, for 2025, they reduced their expectations in terms of deliveries from 33,200 vehicles to 31,400. But they still expect this number to grow to 39,500 by 2029. For the first time ever, the company also announced its projection for 2030. They see the number of school bus deliveries that year hitting 41,700. If this comes to fruition, it would mean even greater growth potential for the company beyond 2028. But only time will tell if that ends up conforming with reality.

Keeping in mind that there is obviously going to be uncertainty about the future, if management can achieve its targets, then shares look even more attractive on a forward basis than they do at the moment. In the table below, I projected out, based on the most recent data provided by management and the firm's current pricing, what revenue and cash flows should look like between now and 2028. The 2028 data looks at the most conservative end of the range that management provided, all the way through the liberal end of the range. And what we see here is that, by that time, the company should be trading at between 6.1 and 6.7 times EBITDA on an EV to EBITDA basis. The price to adjusted operating cash flow multiple should range between 4 and 4.4.

In the next chart below, I wanted to see what kind of upside potential would exist for investors in the event that the company achieves these targets. What I found was that total upside between now and the end of 2028 should be between 34.8% and 49.4%. What gives me confidence here is that this range is pretty small. And in all honesty, I would make the case that, since shares are already undervalued, expansion from a trading multiple perspective is probably warranted as well. As an example, if the firm deserves to trade at an EV to EBITDA multiple of 8 instead of 6, then the conservative forecast for the business would imply upside of 78.7%. The liberal case would result in upside of 98.2%.

The last thing that we should touch on briefly would be management's thoughts about the tariff situation. They acknowledged in the latest earnings call that while they are ‘well-positioned’ to deliver 1,000 EVs this year, instead of the 900 that is currently being guided toward, they may decide to intentionally push some of those builds out into the future since EVs are more heavily exposed to the tariff situation than traditional vehicles. The firm did say that while the company does have a lot of exposure to Canada and Mexico, their products are currently compliant with the USMCA. The company has limited exposure to Europe as well.

Unfortunately, lofty tariffs on steel and aluminum are proving to be problematic. Because they have a steel hedging program covering backlog for this year, they should be okay on that front. But management did say that cost increases could come next year. As for China, the company said that the 145% tariff risks increasing the price of their EVs by over 10%. And as a result, the company will reduce its exposure to the EV space as much as possible until there is a resolution on that front. Management is working with its supply chain partners in order to locate alternative sources of products in the US and elsewhere in North America. And because of the troubles already, it has implemented a 2% increase on the price of all units sold, as well as another 2% rise on all new orders after April 1st of this year. But that was before the tariffs on China rose from 20% to 145%.

Takeaway

At this time, I do believe that there are certain risks associated with the tariff situation for Blue Bird Corporation. But over time, the company has done well and I fully expect that it will continue to do so. Shares are cheap and will be even cheaper in the future based on valuation. Add on top of this the fact that the company has a net cash position of $37.7 million, and I believe that maintaining it as a ‘strong buy’ candidate is the right choice right now.

data.title: Trade Deal With The U.S. Fails To Buoy Sterling Ahead Of The Bank Of England Rate Cut
data.description:
Overview

A couple of days away from the US-China talks, the two are sitting, disputing who sought out the talks. Given the egos, the risk is that the talks are downgraded if not canceled. Expectations ought to be low in any event. On the other hand, the first US trade agreement is expected to be announced with the UK today, though it has not prevented sterling from falling ahead of the Bank of England meeting, which will most likely result in a dovish quarter-point cut. For its part, the greenback is building on yesterday's gains, encouraged by the Federal Reserve's hawkish hold. It is higher against the G10 currencies, and most emerging market currencies.

Equities are mostly firmer. Taiwan's Taiex saw a minor loss, but several bourses in Asia, including India (~-0.5%) and Pakistan (~-6.8%) and a few smaller ones are under pressure. After falling for the past two sessions, Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is recovering, led by the 1%+ rally in the DAX following a 3% surge in Germain industrial production. The S&P and Nasdaq futures are up by over 1%. Bonds are selling off. European benchmark 10-year yields are around three basis points higher though ahead of the BOE meeting and trade deal announcement, Gilts are flat. The 10-year US Treasury yield is up a little more than four basis points to 4.31%. Gold is falling for the second consecutive session. After dropping almost 2% yesterday, the yellow metal is off nearly 0.8% today to about $3340 in Europe. June WTI is up a little more than 1% today to approach $59. A five-day high was set yesterday, a little above $60.

USD: After stopping shy of 100.00 yesterday, the Dollar Index is pushing above it today. A close above 100.00 would discourage the bears, while a move above 100.40 would lend credence to our idea of an upside correction. Reports suggest that the US and UK will announce a trade deal at 10:00 AM ET today. Meanwhile, after President Trump announced on his social media platform yesterday that he will not reduce tariffs on China to ensure successful talks this weekend, China seems to have implied that these are not negotiations but an "engagement." It insists that it is doing, so after the US sought it through various channels. Judging from the president's social media posts, this does not set well there. We suspect that the meeting will either be canceled or amount to practically nothing. Meanwhile, US data is of little consequence today. Nonfarm productivity and unit labor costs are not observed directly but derived from the GDP figures. The contraction in Q1 GDP will translate to a decline in productivity and a rise in unit labor costs. Weekly initial jobless claims are expected to have softened after rising in back-to-back weeks for the first time in a couple of months. In any event, the April employment report eased fears that the deterioration of the labor market was accelerating. As widely expected, the Federal Reserve stood pat yesterday and, despite pressure from the White House, gave no hint that it was preparing to cut rates at the next month's meeting either. The element of hawkishness came from the seeming move away from the possibility that price increases from tariffs might be transitory. The Fed recognized that there are risks on both sides of its mandate, but this does not mean stagflation as some observers claim. Stagflation might be an appropriate label if both risks materialized.

Euro: The euro remained in the upper end of its recent range yesterday but made session lows following the Fed's press conference to almost $1.1290 after it held below $1.1380 earlier. The broader range is about $1.1265-$1.1425. It reached $1.1270 today and is consolidating mostly below $1.1300 in European turnover. The daily momentum indicators are trending lower, but the euro has moved broadly sideways. A convincing break of $1.1260 could signal a two-cent downside correction. Germany reported the first back-to-back increase in industrial production since Jan-Feb 2024. It jumped 3% in March, well above the 1.0% median forecast in Bloomberg's survey, and the most since October 2021. It is not clear how much German factory orders and industrial output was impacted by activity ahead of US tariffs. Moreover, the first estimate of German growth in Q1 was 0.2% after a contraction of the same magnitude in Q4 24.

CNY: The dollar settled higher for the second consecutive session against the offshore yuan and appears to have put in a low. Follow-through buying today lifted it a little above CNH7.24. The next technical target is in the CNH7.25-CNH7.26 area. Since around the middle of March, the PBOC has moved the daily reference rate slightly more than it had been, but since returning from the extended holiday on Tuesday, the PBOC has returned to the smallest of changes in the fix. However, today's fix was set at CNY7.2073 (CNY7.2005 yesterday), the first higher dollar fix in eight sessions, and the largest change in a month. Meanwhile, most Asian currencies fell today, led by the nearly 0.9% pullback in the Malaysian ringgit.

JPY: The decoupling of the exchange rate from the US 10-year yield was underscored by yesterday's price action. The US 10-year yield slipped, while the greenback rose by nearly 1.0% against the yen. It recorded session highs in late dealings yesterday at JPY144.00. The dollar is making session highs in the European session, slightly below JPY145. The daily momentum indicators are still trending up despite the dollar's pullback from JPY146 last week. Although last week's high is the next immediate target, there may be potential toward JPY148.

GBP: Sterling was sold to $1.3280 yesterday after being turned back from $1.3400 on Tuesday, and this is still consistent with a top being carved. It posted an inside day yesterday. Reports about the US trade agreement, after striking one earlier this week with India, helped lift sterling initially to about $1.3355, but it has succumbed to selling pressure and tested key support near $1.3260 in the early European turnover. A break of $1.3260 would lend credence to the bearish take. The 20-day moving average is near $1.3295 and sterling has traded below it for the first time since April 10. The measuring objective of the potential topping pattern could bring it toward $1.3050, which is around the halfway mark of the rally from the April 7 low, a little above $1.27. The swaps market has no doubt that the Bank of England will announce a 25 bps rate cut shortly. Ahead of today's announcement, the market has about a 60% chance of another cut next month. After today's cut, the market has two more cuts fully discounted and almost a 90% chance of another. The weaker economic performance will pose a formidable challenge to Chancellor Reeves’ fiscal plans. The dismal showing of Labour in the recent local elections warns of the public's impatience.

CAD: The greenback recovered against the Canadian dollar yesterday. After setting a new low for the year on Tuesday (~CAD1.3750), the US dollar reached almost CAD1.3840 yesterday. Today, it has reached almost CAD1.3885 and is above the 20-day moving average (~CAD1.3850) for the first time since April 2. We suspect corrective potential may extend toward CAD1.40. It is a quiet session for Canadian data today, but the focus is on tomorrow's job report. After losing 32.6k jobs in March, Canada is expected to have added about 5k jobs last month, according to the median in Bloomberg survey. However, the 13 projections ranged from a loss of 40k jobs to a gain of 51k. The Bank of Canada meets on June 4 and the swap market looks split about the outlook. Still, another cut is fully discounted by the end of July when it meets next.

AUD: The Australian dollar recorded a bearish key reversal yesterday by making a new high ($0.6515) and then falling through and settling below Tuesday's low (slightly below $0.6440). It is hard to separate the greenback's advance in response to the hawkish hold by the Fed and President Trump's announcement that there would be not pulling back from the 145% tariff on China. Initial support near $0.6400 is holding so far today, but if a larger correction is underway, a move toward $0.6285 initially seems reasonable. The 20-day moving average, which the Aussie has not traded below since April 14 is nearly $0.6395 today.

MXN: The peso was unexpectedly resilient yesterday. On Tuesday, the dollar looked like it was breaking out of the extended consolidation. It traded above MXN19.78, its highest level in nearly three weeks. Yesterday, the greenback fell a little below MXN19.56. It is trading in a narrow range between roughly MXN19.56 and MXN19.6230. President Sheinbaum seemed optimistic about her relationship with Trump. Yet, Trump's endorsement Tuesday of the USMCA he negotiated in is firm term is a bit disingenuous. The tariffs he has announced appear to violate the treaty. Maybe his comments were misunderstood. The USMCA treaty is to be re-examined next year. Trump said might be unnecessary, but that is because of US unilateralism. We suspect that the US might be more interested in two bilateral agreements rather than North American Free-Trade Agreement. Ironically, the peso fared better than the Brazilian real, which fell by about 0.50% yesterday ahead of the central bank's decision to hike the Selic rate by 50 bps to 14.75%. The Deputy Governor of Banxico played up the scope for rate cuts. Mexico's central bank is expected to deliver another 50 bps rate cut next week.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Economy Is Finally Cracking: Here's How To Rebalance Your Portfolio
data.description:
Introduction

2025 has been an eventful year so far, with tariff uncertainty causing stocks to sell-off and enter into correction territory in early April.

Although the market has recovered its losses since, the economy is finally starting to show cracks. Going forward, I expect we could see a further sell-off as recession fears continue to rise.

Due to potential stagflation, many companies' earnings could see pressure in the coming months, which could put investors on high alert.

But this should be seen as an opportunity to double-down on some of your favorite stocks.

Instead of selling and sitting on the sidelines in cash to wait out the storm, in this article, I give investors ideas on how they should position their portfolios to continue growing their income.

Slower Growth Likely To Continue

During the first quarter, GDP growth slowed for the first time in 3 years. Is this a sign the economy is finally starting to show cracks? In my opinion, yes.

Additionally, I think we have a high likelihood of seeing another quarter of negative growth, which technically would mean we're in a recession.

Until then, the market will likely remain relatively flat while experiencing some volatility in between.

Of course, one quarter of negative growth is not the end all be all, but I've felt higher for longer interest rates have weighed on consumers for quite some time now.

In the chart below you can see how mortgage, home, student loan, auto, and credit card debt have all risen over the past year, moving into the serious delinquency category.

Despite consumer spending seemingly remaining resilient since 2022, the lagging effect of interest rates is starting to show up and will continue going forward.

And collections for delinquent student loans, originally paused during the Trump administration that continued during the Biden presidency, have also resumed this month.

With interest rates remaining higher for longer, slowing economic growth, and massive lay-offs, our recession odds are pretty high if you ask me.

Something else that made me really believe the economy is starting to crack is Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) also suffered their worst quarter since COVID, where they saw a 32% decline in the third (quarter). Management recently stated uncertainty surrounding trade policies remains a risk.

Buffett likely wasn't surprised by the result, as he's been increasing his cash pile in recent years. It's like he's been preparing for a recession.

As a result, stocks will likely sell off as investors grow worried about losing their retirement savings. However, I'm a firm believer in staying invested no matter the circumstances.

As Peter Lynch has been quoted saying:

If you like a stock it at $14 and it goes to $6, that's great. You understand the company, you look at the balance sheet, and they're doing fine. You're hoping to get to $22 with it, $14 to $22 is terrific, $6 to $22 is exceptional. So, you take advantage of these declines.

He's also stated the most important organ is the stomach and that volatility is terrific. While I think it's important to stay invested, you should also consider repositioning your portfolio if a downturn is expected. Take a more defensive approach.

Without further ado, let's get into how investors should consider positioning their portfolios.

Weighting Towards REITs

Back in 2022 / 2023 when REITs (XLRE) were out of favor due to interest rates rapidly rising, many suffered steep share price declines.

I remember penning buy articles on REITs like Agree Realty (ADC), Realty Income (O), VICI Properties (VICI), & Simon Property Group (SPG).

Many argued that REITs were horrible investments, even uninvestable, as a result of their below-average returns.

Despite their higher than average yields, investors seemed to bail on the sector and instead opted for high-yield investments like Business Development Companies (BIZD).

But below you can see the total returns of these REITs vs the S&P (SP500) in the past 365 days.

With the exception of Realty Income, all have beaten the index in total returns with ADC leading the way at 37.36%, followed by SPG at 20.24%, compared to the S&P's 12.29%.

Past performance is no indicator of future performance, but I suspect these REITs to continue to perform well and potentially beat the index in total returns. Why?

Because of their highly diversified portfolios, particularly Agree Realty who has the highest exposure to investment-rated and essential tenants like Walmart (WMT), Costco (COST), & Home Depot (HD) at 68%.

Their CEO has also been preparing the REIT to capitalize on investment opportunities should they arise by deleveraging their balance sheet and increasing liquidity. This stood at just 2.8x, with a total of $2.2 billion in liquidity.

I'm not necessarily saying buy Agree Realty as they have the highest multiple with a forward P/AFFO of 17.82x, but their monthly dividend, robust liquidity, and investment-rated tenant exposure make them a viable option.

They are likely to provide price stability during a downturn as a result. ADC also pay monthly, which helps investors compound faster.

Another option is looking into Healthcare REITs like CareTrust REIT (CTRE) & Sila Realty Trust (SILA).

Although a downturn serves as a risk to any stock, particularly REITs as tenants could stop paying rent or occupancies could decline and impact their financials in the process. Those with higher retail tenant quality or who lease to healthcare tenants are likely to do well.

In this article by Investopedia, REITs, Healthcare (XLV), and Consumer Staples (XLP) are all sectors expected to thrive during downturns.

And this is obviously due to their importance to the economy. No matter what's happening, consumers still need essential items like groceries, auto work, and to take care of their healthcare needs.

Consider Buying Low-Cost ETFs

When it comes to investing, my philosophy has always been quality over quantity. I consider myself boring, so that's why I never chase the high-flying stocks in the Technology sector (XLK).

Instead, I opted for exposure to the sector through low-cost ETFs like Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG) & the SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG). Both funds have expense ratios of just 0.04% and 0.02%, respectively.

They also have exposure to some of the largest technology companies in the world, like Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and NVIDIA Corp. (NVDA).

SCHG's exposure was higher at roughly 45%. This is also a reason why the fund has a much lower dividend yield compared to SPLG at just 0.43% vs 1.34% for the latter.

SPLG's tech exposure was nearly 31%. However, SPLG has a broader portfolio with 503 holdings, more than double SCHG's 229 holdings.

At the current prices, SCHG trades slightly below their NAV price of $26.10 at $26 while SPLG trades above their NAV price of $65.71 at $66.73. Last month, both funds traded at a significant discount as they sold off heavily after Liberation Day on April 2nd.

While I think both are worth buying at current levels, investors should look for increased volatility in the coming months and add to both below their NAV prices.

There are other high-quality, more popular funds like Vanguard's S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR's other S&P 500 ETF (SPY), and Invesco's ETF (QQQ). But all funds are much more expensive with slightly higher expense ratios compared to SCHG & SPLG.

VOO's expense ratio is comparable and slightly lower than SCHG's at 0.03%. But the significantly higher price tags for each make the two funds more attractive options in my opinion.

Risks & Investor Takeaway

Investors should consider rebalancing towards REITs & low-cost ETFs in preparation for a potential recession.

However, REITs and ETFs could see a sell-off as well if we do see a recession. And this is something investors should consider when looking to rebalance their portfolios.

Furthermore, although REITs have dividend yields that could be considered attractive, I would look for those with high exposure to essential tenants and avoid REITs with exposure to offices, malls, and experiential assets due to slower consumer spending.

ETFs will likely do well as they provide instant diversification. But investors should look for those with low-costs, particularly with expense ratios below 1%.

Also, avoid high-yielding, options ETFs as these typically have much higher expense ratios despite and suffer NAV erosion while paying out high income on a monthly basis.

Instead of looking to sell out and wait for a better entry, consider weighing your portfolio towards REITs and ETFs as they not only provide higher than average yields, but stability & reliability.

data.title: Reading International: Real Estate Sales Unlock Hidden Value
data.description:
Introduction and Business Overview

It’s been over nine years since authoring my last Seeking Alpha article on Reading International (NASDAQ: RDI) (“Important Takeaways From Reading International’s 2015 Annual Meeting”). Since then, the company has weathered a pandemic, movie industry strikes, and a generational shift in moviegoing habits. Through it all, Reading’s deep bench of real estate, painstakingly assembled over decades by the late Jim Cotter, Sr., has quietly preserved and created value for shareholders.

Today, with RDI shares near all-time lows, the market’s discount to asset value is more visible than ever. Through recent and pending property sales, Reading is converting its “hidden” value into cash and using the proceeds to reduce debt, making its balance sheet more transparent. This article focuses on the value in Reading’s real estate, and the visible deleveraging and reduction in interest expense occurring from announced asset sales that will benefit the company regardless of the pace and scope of the rebound in Reading’s cinema segment.

For those new to my Seeking Alpha articles on Reading International, the company operates two primary business segments: cinema exhibition and real estate. As of Reading’s recent year-end 2024 10-K, the company owns and operates 60 cinemas (486 screens at year-end 2024) across the United States, Australia, and New Zealand, under brands such as Reading Cinemas, Consolidated Theatres, and Angelika Film Center. In Australia and New Zealand, Reading Cinemas maintains #4 and #3 market shares, respectively, with a strong presence in major cities and as an anchor tenant in synergistic shopping centers.

The Real Estate Segment includes both income-producing retail and commercial properties, most notably, entertainment-themed shopping centers (ETRCs) in Australia and New Zealand, and long-held, appreciated parcels in New York City (Cinemas 1,2,3, 44 Union Square, Minetta Lane; Orpheum), as well as other high-value, developable properties now being monetized or repositioned. At 12/31/24, before taking into account subsequent property sales discussed below, Reading’s total book value of assets was $471 million, with a market cap under $40 million and net debt of $187 million, resulting in an enterprise value of just $227 million, far below the sum of its parts.

Real Estate Monetization and Balance Sheet Transformation

During the pandemic and subsequent industry turmoil, Reading generated over $150 million in proceeds from the sale of non-core and underutilized properties (Manukau, Coachella, Auburn Redyard Centre, Royal George Theater, Invercargill, Maitland, and Culver City), often at substantial gains. These funds helped cover losses from COVID and the movie industry’s labor shutdowns, and also supported a modest reduction in debt. As a result, Reading exited the industry’s most challenging period with slightly lower net debt than it had pre-Pandemic, and, most importantly, without meaningful equity dilution to shareholders. This stands in stark contrast to peers: AMC Entertainment (AMC) and Cinemark (CNK) issued substantial new equity or convertible debt, respectively, to survive, while Cineworld/Regal (CINE.L) went through bankruptcy and massive dilution. Reading’s real estate asset base enabled it to navigate industry turbulence without major equity issuance. Its share count (RDI and NASDAQ:RDIB combined) has remained essentially flat in the 22–23 million range, with only minor increases from equity compensation.

The most impactful transaction is the January 2025 sale of Courtenay Central in Wellington, New Zealand, which generated NZ$38 million (US$23.5 million). Per the Reading Q4 2024 earnings release and Q4 2024 Earnings audiocast transcript, this sale eliminated all of the company’s New Zealand debt and, for the first time, Reading was able to repatriate capital to the US to pay down $6.1 million of its Bank of America US term loan, materially reducing this very high-rate near-term maturing balance.

Critically, the transaction included a long-term leaseback of Reading’s Courtenay Central multiplex. This was previously Reading’s highest-grossing cinema in New Zealand and one of the top cinemas nationwide until closed in early 2019 due to seismic concerns in the building. Earlier in 2024, the City of Wellington backed out of a proposed sale plus buy-back arrangement that would have provided Reading, in effect, with a low-cost loan to repair and reopen the center, but only after several years and with significant additional capital required from Reading. Now, the developer who purchased the shopping center and adjoining parcels from Reading will fully fund and is expected to complete the costly seismic upgrades by mid-2026. This could enable Reading to refurbish the cinema with state-of-the-art seating, projection, and sound, and reopen it in a resurgent Wellington entertainment district as early as the end of 2026.

Similarly, Reading is now under contract to sell the Cannon Park Shopping Centre in Townsville, Australia, for A$32 million (US$21 million), with closing expected in Q2 2025. Per Reading’s 2024 10-K, sale proceeds will retire a meaningful chunk of the company’s Australian debt. This transaction also includes a leaseback of the Reading Cinemas multi-plex anchoring the center, ensuring the company retains a substantial portion of the cash flow this shopping center currently generates for Reading.

Smaller monetization efforts are also underway with a 24-acre industrial railroad adjacent parcel in Williamsport, PA (owned since before 1900!) and some potential cinema property sale-leasebacks that could provide low- to mid-single-digit millions in proceeds. These transactions share a common thread: converting underutilized real estate into debt reduction. Moreover, where there is a Reading Cinema, retaining those operations preserves cash flow.

While the timing of these smaller efforts is undetermined, the Wellington and Cannon Park deals alone will remove almost $45 million of debt from the balance sheet in just the two upcoming quarters.

Retained Real Estate: Key Value Pillars

Even after these sales, Reading’s remaining real estate portfolio is anchored by high-value, income-producing assets in New York, Australia, and New Zealand, with additional long-term optionality in Philadelphia. A table summarizing key remaining assets is shown below.

Valuation Methodology Note: All property values discussed below are author estimates based on a combination of company-reported net book values (from 10-K filings), historical bids, comparable asset sales, and management commentary where available. Actual realized values may differ. These are not formal appraisals.

Key Real Estate Assets
(USD; Excluding Cannon Park Centre - under contract for sale)

Property/Asset Location Net Book Value

Estimated Value

 Notes
44 Union Square New York $95M $100M+
Cinemas 1,2,3 (75% ownership) New York $24M $45-60M redevelopment value
NewMarket Village & Contiguous Office Bldgs Brisbane $39 $40-45M+
Other ETRCs/Office Bldgs AU & NZ $13M $15-18M
Orpheum & Minetta Lane Theaters New York $3M $6-10M redevelopment value
Reading Viaduct & Adjacent Parcels Philadelphia $0-2M $10-15M redevelopment value
Totals $176M $216-248M+

The current value of these retained assets is not simply a matter of net book value, which totals approximately $176 million (excluding Cannon Park Centre). Many have appreciated over time. Some parcels, such as Cinemas 1,2,3, the Orpheum and Minetta Lane Theaters in NYC, not only have long holding periods but also offer significantly higher alternative use and redevelopment value, should the company choose or be compelled to sell.

For example, 44 Union Square was formerly the Union Square Theater (and before that Tammany Hall) until it was redeveloped into a 73,000 sq. ft., six-floor mixed-use building. Petco’s new flagship store leases the lower specialty retail floors of this award-winning building, while efforts continue to lease the upper floors, likely to office tenants, as the market for prime locations, like Union Square, rebounds. Once fully leased, this building should generate $6–8 million in NOI, supporting a higher estimated value and helping to de-risk a refinancing at much lower rates than the current 12.5% mortgage.

Reading also holds a 75% controlling ownership interest in the Cinemas 1,2,3 property on Third Avenue in New York. With current zoning rights to redevelop this parcel into a 10-story, 96,000 sq. ft mixed-use building (potentially more with a favorable variance), the site, as a redevelopment, has been valued by past 2012 unsolicited bids, other analysts, and market comparables, at $45–60 million, well above book value. Given this parcel’s prime location on New York City’s Upper East Side, directly across from Bloomingdale’s and right by a subway stop, I believe that the current redevelopment value is even higher. However, for purposes of this thesis, we don’t need to stretch.

Following the sale of the Wellington parcels and pending sale of the Cannon Park Shopping Centre, NewMarket Village and adjacent properties in Brisbane will become Reading’s most valuable remaining asset outside the U.S. It has high occupancy, strong rental income, and upside redevelopment potential, especially with the contiguous office building now under Reading’s ownership. Other smaller Australian and New Zealand ETRCs, like Belmont Common and Napier, are also fully leased and income-producing, Reading Cinemas as anchor tenants.

I remain conservative in valuing the Philadelphia Reading Viaduct and adjacent parcels. The City of Philadelphia adopted an ordinance to acquire the actual Viaduct parcel via Eminent Domain and incorporate it into an extension of the city’s Rail Park (Philly’s version of the NYC’s High Line). While management’s initial asking price is purportedly $50 million, any condemnation would require a court to determine “fair value”. For now, Reading has successfully delayed the city’s efforts, asserting that the Viaduct, an old vestige from the Reading Railroad (yes from Monopoly fame) remains under Federal Surface Transportation Board jurisdiction.

This legal delay may ultimately enhance value, as the surrounding area, including Reading’s multiple small parcels along the old tracks, is the focus of redevelopment and improvement (e.g. the Chinatown Stitch project). While I estimate the Viaduct’s current value is only a fraction of Reading’s $50 million ask, Reading’s parcels in this area offer meaningful long-term optionality.

Segment Performance and Outlook

Historically, Reading’s Cinema Segment has been the company’s largest cash flow generator. However, in recent years, as the Cinema Segment was weakened by the pandemic and labor strikes, the Real Estate Segment has provided some cash flow stability. Recent results confirm the resilience and contribution of the real estate segment. In Q4 2024, Reading’s global real estate revenues and operating income year-over-year rose 14% and 150%, respectively. Full year global real estate revenues and operating income for 2024 increased to $20.0 million and $4.7 million, respectively, supported by higher rental and ancillary income and a 96% leasing rate for non-cinema rental space. Even as underperforming properties were sold, core real estate assets in Australia and New Zealand maintained high occupancy, with most tenants continuing to meet rental obligations. This operational resilience underpins the company’s valuation.

Valuation: Retained Real Estate Exceeds Pro Forma Enterprise Value

With the Wellington and Cannon Park sales, Reading will have converted $24 million of 12/31/24 net book value into nearly $45 million of sale proceeds and debt reduction in just two quarters. Even after these sales, the remaining real estate portfolio, including 44 Union Square, Cinemas 1,2,3, NewMarket Village, and other Australian/NZ assets, is, in my opinion, conservatively worth $215 million or more. This is well above the company’s current pro forma Enterprise Value, which, reflecting net debt reduction from these two major sales, is likely to fall below $190 million (calculated as 12/31/24 net debt of $186.7 million, less $44.5MM in combined sales proceeds, plus $47.6 in combined RDI and RDIB market value as of 5/2/25).

At current prices, investors are effectively getting Reading’s entire cinema business for free.

Pro forma Enterprise Value:

$190M

Retained Real Estate Value:

$215-248M

Difference/Cinema Value

>($25M) = (FREE!)

Cash Flow and Liquidity Risks: Addressed By Asset Sales

While Reading International (RDI) is a microcap and has reported net losses, a substantial portion of those losses are non-cash depreciation due to its real estate holdings. The company reported positive EBITDA in Q4 2024, and trailing twelve-month EBITDA is also positive. Moreover, trailing twelve-month EBITDA will continue to rise as I believe any seasonally negative EBITDA in Q1 25 (not sure even if it will be negative) will be much improved from Q1 2024’s large movie industry strike-impacted negative performance.

Furthermore, the $44 million in real estate sale proceeds, discussed above, greatly reduce near-term debt maturities, lower future cash interest expense, and improve the ability to refinance other debt. Reading’s liquidity position is significantly improved.

Catalysts: From Visibility to Recognition

Reading’s Q1 and Q2 2025 results will provide the latest clear evidence of real estate value realization, with net debt reduction, lower interest expense, and improved liquidity. Further lease-up and refinancing of 44 Union Square, potential redevelopment of other assets, and ongoing asset sales all provide additional upside. Management is also now renewing investor outreach. Reading is scheduled to present at the Sidoti Micro-Cap Virtual conference on Thursday, May 22, at 10:45 AM EDT and conduct one-on-one meetings before and after this presentation. Management has also indicated plans to hold non-deal roadshows later this year, likely with Macquarie (currently the only brokerage covering RDI, with an Outperform rating and $2.50/share target). These activities may further help close the persistent valuation gap.

Conclusion: A Margin of Safety at an Inflection Point with Optionality

Reading’s ability to avoid the kind of equity dilution seen at AMC, Cinemark, and Cineworld/Regal is a direct result of its real estate asset base. The market has long overlooked the hidden value embedded in Reading’s balance sheet, but that value continues to be realized through a series of monetizations. With major asset sales closing and overall cash flow turning positive again, debt reduction will accelerate, and Reading’s compelling investment thesis will finally become visible in the numbers.

Even after these significant asset sales close, high-value properties remain on the books, worth more than Reading’s current enterprise value. As the market begins to focus more on net debt, a metric far easier to assess than the value of assets classified as “held for sale”, RDI’s deep discount to intrinsic value should begin to close. This revaluation also sets the stage for further upside, as the movie exhibition industry, and Reading’s Cinema Segment with it, continues its intertwined rebound.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hecla Mining: Further Weakness Should Present A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tons per day. UG = Underground.

Q1 Production & Sales

Hecla Mining (NYSE:HL) released its Q1-25 results last week, reporting quarterly production of ~4.1 million ounces of silver and ~34,200 ounces of gold. This translated to a ~5% decline in silver production and ~7% decline in gold production from the year-ago period, impacted by lower gold grades at Casa Berardi (Quebec, Canada), lower grades and throughput at Greens Creek (Alaska), offset by record throughput and slightly higher grades at Lucky Friday.

Overall, Q1-25 production was in line with plan, though, with backfill and development activities (an issue in Q4-25 at Greens Creek) improving throughout the quarter, according to Hecla.

Greens Creek

Starting with Greens Creek, production came in at ~2.0 million ounces of silver, down from ~2.48 million ounces in the year-ago period. As noted, this was related to lower grades (11.75 ounce per ton silver vs. 13.3 ounce per ton of silver), offset by higher base metals grades, as well as lower throughput. On a positive note, the mine still generated over $33 million in mine-site free cash flow and AISC declined to [-] $0.03/oz after by-product credits, an improvement from $7.16/oz in the year-ago period.

That being said, labor/power costs will be higher this year and costs are expected to increase due to increased diesel power usage related to maintenance at the hydropower facility powering Greens Creek. So, while margins were impressive in Q1-25 and will remain solid given high gold by-product credits, we will see some catch-up in sustaining capital that will impact AISC in the coming quarters and elevated diesel power usage (higher cost power).

Looking at Lucky Friday, quarterly production came in at ~1.33 million ounces (Q1-24: ~1.06 million ounces), benefiting from record mill throughput (~108,800 tons) at slightly higher grades (13.0 ounces per ton of silver and 12.2% lead/zinc). Unfortunately, AISC came in higher year-over-year at $20.08/oz (Q1-24: $17.36/oz) and free cash flow was just $8.4 million, partially impacted by higher capex.

Hecla noted that higher costs were related to increased profit sharing payments, higher consumables costs and higher costs for drill contractors. The company has since implemented cost control initiatives that are aimed at reducing reliance on high-cost contractors. As for cost guidance, it has been revised up to $20.75/oz (mid-point) vs. $17.25/oz previously, related to higher labor costs, higher insurance costs than budget, profit-share, increased contractor usage and prices for some consumables.

Keno Hill

As for Keno Hill, production came in at ~772,000 ounces of silver on the back of slightly higher throughput (~27,400 tons processed) at higher grades just shy of 30 ounces per ton of silver. Unfortunately, the mine still saw a free cash outflow of $18.4 million, has yet to reach commercial production and has a more difficult year in 2025 with power curtailments impacting Q1-25 results because of powerline maintenance by YEC, and repairs scheduled later this year, resulting in some minor downtime in August 2025.

Assuming these were the only issues for Keno Hill, this would be best to ignore. However, as the company has shared, mill throughput levels continue to be well below permitted capacity (305 TPD vs. 440 TPD), with tons mined even further below capacity and this is an asset that needs 600 TPD to be profitable according to Hecla. Unfortunately, this has left the company miles away from generating any meaningful free cash flow and the company is in a tough spot given that many of the keys to hitting consistent free cash flow generation are out of its control, with it stating below:

Keno Hill has generated marginal profits for the company at current throughput rates and prices. Our immediate focus is to advance permits and successfully execute infrastructure projects, with the goal of putting the mine on a path toward achieving its current permitted capacity of 440 tons per day which, at current prices, is expected to generate positive free cash flow. We estimate that to be sustainably profitable at our current long-range metals prices (which are significantly lower than current prices), throughput rates would need to reach approximately 500 to 600 tons per day, due to Keno Hill's high fixed costs.

Currently, Keno Hill is not configured to sustainably produce 440 tons per day (although the mill has achieved that rate for multiple weeks on end during test run periods). Achieving 440 or higher tons per day would require ore from both the Bermingham deposit and the lower grade Flame & Moth deposit, significant capital expenditures, obtaining permits, executing projects, mine development and maintaining community support. If any one of these were not to occur, particularly if prices were to decrease from current prices, Keno Hill as currently configured would not be profitable, and placing the operation on care and maintenance would be an option.

This is far from the most upbeat language for this new asset it acquired with its Alexco acquisition, especially with this asset now over two years into its planned turnaround and despite a tailwind from much higher silver prices. Further, commercial production is still a way's off, with just one of five criteria met today, with this being silver recoveries at expected state production levels, while much work needs to be done in the throughput, capex, and operational commissioning departments.

None of this means that Keno Hill can't be a profitable asset for the company in due course, but it's looking like a sustained period below $30.00/oz silver could take this asset offline and move it into care and maintenance, and other factors (timely receipt of permitting) could also potentially derail production. This isn't the end of the world, but this certainly isn't language one wants to hear when another asset is also nearing a production cliff in 2027 and potentially up for sale.

Unfortunately, it's not as simple as ramping up Bermingham, Flame & Moth given that it needs permits for this, and with waste/tailings storage set to reach capacity by 2028, permit timing has become more sensitive. Further, even if they were to accelerate production currently, it runs out of space, which puts even more pressure on permitting timelines. In summary, while the future post 2028 may be bright for this asset, I think it's best to assume negative free cash flow generation without a sustained $40.00/oz silver price for this operation until at least 2027 while it operates at well below planned levels, with hopefully better results post-2027.

Casa Berardi

Finally, Casa Berardi produced ~20,500 ounces at $2,303/oz AISC (Q1-24: ~22,000 ounces at $1,899/oz AISC), with higher throughput offset by lower grades (0.06 ounces per ton of gold vs. 0.07 ounces per ton of gold). While costs are higher, Hecla expects costs to improve in Q3 with a further improvement in Q4, as it sees a decline in its strip ratio. Unfortunately, despite record gold prices near $2,900/oz, the asset still didn't generate any mine-site free cash flow in the period (Q1-25: [-] $6.4 million).

As for the asset's future, production will continue from the 160 Pit until 2027 and the asset is expected to see a meaningful improvement in free cash flow later this year. However, with a multi-year gap in production from 2027 until 2032, Hecla continues to look at strategic alternatives for this asset that could include:

a] an outright sale

b] a joint venture

c] a spin-out

d] extending the UG mine

e] accelerating future cash flow to capture a portion of record gold prices with a prepayment structure or financing arrangement

The company noted on its Q1-25 Conference Call that work is still underway and that it should be able to report on this "by next quarter, if not sooner".

Costs & Margins

As for costs and margins, Hecla's silver AISC came in at $11.91/oz in Q1-25, an improvement from $13.10/oz in the year-ago period because of higher by-product credits. This translated to ~64.5% AISC margins ($21.68/oz) vs. $11.67/oz (~47.1%) in the year-ago period, benefiting from a higher realized silver price of $33.59/oz. While these were industry-leading AISC margins and its cost profile remains well below that of its silver peers, Hecla has reiterated guidance of ~$16.25/oz AISC, suggesting costs will creep up as the year progresses, and it catches up on sustaining capital spend.

Unfortunately, its gold segment didn't fare nearly as well, with AISC margins of just 21.7% despite a $2,940/oz gold price. However, and as explained previously, Casa Berardi is not a core asset and even if it is kept in the portfolio, we should see far better costs later in the year and a swing back to positive free cash flow. So, while free cash flow was negative in Q1-25, I would still expect Hecla to generate at least $120 million in free cash flow this year, helping it to start to de-lever and invest more in its exploration assets as it works to replace lost production post-2027 from Casa Berardi.

On that note, it has started drilling at its high-grade Midas/Hollister past-producing mines in Nevada, with this potentially offering an opportunity for a late decade restart if gold prices are supportive, and it sees exploration success. This could potentially help to offset the production cliff at Casa Berardi, adding more Tier-1 jurisdiction gold ounces, though a few years too late to provide a clean offset for this production cliff.

As for its Libby Project in Montana (acquired with the Mines Management acquisition in 2016), the recent FAST-41 designation that aims to provide a more streamlined permitting process for this silver-copper deposit is a positive development. Still, significant work needs to be completed here to assess this opportunity before this could asset could move into commercial production, with this a 2032 or later opportunity, if approved. In fact, assuming an updated Plan of Operations by year-end, Hecla still needs to complete significant de-watering, with the company noting that half of its existing ~4,200-meter adit is underwater, with more underground development needed to start exploration.

So, while it's certainly positive that the market might start giving Hecla a little more credit for its exploration portfolio with the potential for drills to be turning in Nevada and Montana, neither of the opportunities will move into production quick enough to smooth the production cliff set to be left by Casa Berardi.

Valuation

Based on ~637 million fully diluted shares and a share price of US$4.55, Hecla trades at a market cap of ~$2.90 billion and an enterprise value of ~$3.42 billion. This sharp decline in market cap has left it trading at a discount to peers like First Majestic (AG) with an inferior jurisdictional profile and a far shorter weighted average mine life, in addition to a far less impressive exploration portfolio. That said, First Majestic is easily the most overvalued stock in the silver space, so while HL is certainly very attractively valued relative to AG, this is not that meaningful of an argument.

As for how Hecla stacks up vs. peers, HL continues to sit near the top of the pack from an EV/EBITDA multiple standpoint, largely related to its premium multiple given that it's the only silver producer with a Tier-1 only jurisdictional profile. And in a world where the less attractive jurisdictions aren't getting any easier to operate/invest in (renegotiation of royalties/ownership, occasional nationalization, lack of community support), Hecla's position as a Tier-1 only producer certainly makes it stand out from the pack with this being one of the most important attributes to have, especially in a rising precious metals environment where greed can set in.

Plus, it certainly doesn't hurt to own one of the highest grade and longest-life silver assets globally (Greens Creek and Lucky Friday), with this being another unique attribute that is harder to find in the silver space (*). This is especially true when primarily silver assets are getting more difficult to find and with fewer available for purchase after Coeur (CDE) and First Majestic (AG) jumped at the opportunity to pick up two of these assets last year, paying through the nose to ensure they could add silver exposure to their portfolio.

(*) While higher grade, Las Chispas, San Dimas, Santa Elena, and Guanacevi are shorter life assets (*)

Using what I believe to be fair multiples of 1.5x P/NAV (6%) and 14.0x forward cash flow for a silver producer operating solely out of Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions, I see an updated fair value for Hecla of US$6.10. This points to a 34% upside from current levels, but I am looking for a minimum 25% discount to fair value for Tier-1 only producers and believe that a 30% discount is more warranted here given the less ideal visibility into the future production profile (Casa Berardi Sale? Keno Hill Permits?).

Based on this required 30% discount to fair value, the updated low-risk buy zone for Hecla comes in at US$4.27. As for its technical picture, HL hasn't given a confirmed technical buy signal yet, with it not likely to give one unless it heads below US$4.30.

Summary

Hecla reported a slightly weaker Q1 than expected, with Greens Creek outperforming on costs (where cost guidance has since been reduced), offset by higher costs at Lucky Friday (where cost guidance has been revised higher). Meanwhile, its financial results were also weaker than I anticipated due partially to production ramping up later in the quarter, resulting in negative working capital adjustments with the company not able to sell all its inventory, nor collect some funds for product sold.

This is expected to reverse and set up a stronger Q2-25 financially, and while Casa Berardi (Hecla's one gold mine in Quebec) has been no help from a free cash flow standpoint despite record gold prices, this will also reverse in Q3-25 with a lower strip ratio at its 160 Pit. Hence, assuming metals prices hold up at current levels, Hecla should have a much stronger second half of the year from a free cash flow standpoint and it's possible we could see a sale of Casa Berardi which would help the company to de-lever, with it continuing to have one of the weaker balance sheets among its mid-tier peers (~$540 million in net debt).

On a negative note, the path to free cash flow at Keno has been much more difficult than expected at the time of this acquisition. In fact, Hecla has noted that this asset needs static silver prices, favorable permitting timelines, higher mine production rates and community support to be consistently profitable, and it's still not yet in commercial production. This is not ideal because there is now low visibility around two projects (Casa Berardi offline in 2027 and potentially sold near-term? / Keno Hill possible care and maintenance?) if silver prices can't hold above $32.00/oz or if it doesn't see the timely receipt of permits.

This is not a huge deal given that the stock has little value assigned to these two assets, but it has created a potential production cliff depending on how things go for Keno over the next 18-24 months.

Overall, I believe we have seen the worst of the correction for HL with the stock dropping to US$4.50, but with more conservative assumptions around Keno Hill, HL's updated low-risk buy zone comes in at US$4.27, and the stock still has not given a confirmed technical buy signal.

This doesn't mean that the stock can't go higher, but I would need a pullback closer to US$4.30 to consider moving the stock back into my portfolio. For now, I continue to see more attractive bets elsewhere for silver exposure.

data.title: Clarion Partners Real Estate Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund summary

Clarion Partners Real Estate Income Fund (the “Fund”) is a daily valued, continuously offered ‘40 Act real estate fund that holds a strategically diversified portfolio of real estate assets across property types and geographic regions. The Fund is managed by Clarion Partners, a registered investment adviser with 1,460 properties and $72.5 billion in real estate assets under management. 1

Fund strategy

The Fund seeks to generate attractive current income with the potential for long-term appreciation and favorable risk-adjusted returns by investing in a portfolio of private commercial real estate and publicly traded real estate securities. The Fund offers direct access to institutional-quality private real estate via Clarion’s real estate platform, tenured investment team and deep understanding of macro and fundamental real estate dynamics gained over many market cycles.

The Pearl at Boulder Creek is a senior loan originated for the recapitalization of a 116-unit senior living community in Boulder, CO, located adjacent to the University of Colorado Boulder campus. The experienced Sponsors include an institutional investment management firm and a leading Senior Housing operator.

Boulder is a scenic retirement destination whose 80+ population cohort is forecast to expand 6.3% annually over the next five years, substantially above the national average of 4.7%. Clarion believes the market is also subject to significant barriers to entry which insulate the Property from future competition from new supply.3

The Property’s unit mix consists of one- and two-bedroom units for both independent living and assisted living that provide accommodation to appeal to a broad range of prospective residents. Units offer modern features including high-ceilings, laundry, balconies, large closets and upscale kitchens with natural stone counter tops and stainless-steel appliances. Community amenities include driver service to shopping, events and medical appointments, outdoor patio spaces, lounge and game rooms, a salon and nail spa, indoor pool, full-service dining room, on-site restaurant, fitness center, and an art studio.

U.S. market update

Recent economic data suggests that the U.S. economy remains on solid footing. However, considering the market’s reaction to the recent tariff announcements, the likelihood for a deceleration in these positive trends has risen. As of March, total employment increased by a relatively healthy 228,000 jobs and the unemployment rate remained near full employment at just above 4%. 5 The Fed is signaling for two rate cuts this year due in large part to the economy’s slowing expansion and the gradual deceleration in inflation. However, threat of a renewed trade war and the rising probability of a recession have clouded the near-term interest rate and economic outlook. Although uncertainty remains elevated, the resilience of the U.S. economy and expectations for lower interest rates are positives for commercial real estate

Following a period of elevated interest rates and a challenging capital markets environment, commercial real estate transaction activity, although still relatively subdued, appears to be improving. 6 Additional Fed rate cuts and a subsequent lowering in debt costs may support sales volume and valuations in 2025, although uncertainty around the volatility of long-term rates, escalating geopolitical tensions, tariffs and immigration may keep some investors on the sidelines.

Tighter lending conditions and elevated construction costs have led to a substantial reduction in construction starts across property types, which should support solid NOI growth going forward. 7 Our investment strategy remains focused on sectors that are poised to benefit from favorable, structural demand tailwinds, bolstering tenant demand during periods of economic turbulence and rising uncertainty.

Fund highlights

The Fund completed three acquisitions during the first quarter. These include an industrial outdoor storage (IOS) investment in Houston, TX, one senior loan on a national portfolio of IOS investments, and one senior loan on a senior living community in Boulder, CO, highlighted on the previous page.

Houston Airport IOS is a 100% leased, 7.59-acre IOS facility in Houston, TX. The Property is located directly off Beltway 8, proximate to the area’s major distribution throughfares including the George Bush Intercontinental Airport. The Property was fully renovated in 2024 and is improved with a 23k sf warehouse, offering tenants flexibility for fluctuating logistics needs.

The National IOS Portfolio is a senior loan for the recapitalization of an eight- property portfolio located across seven MSAs: Chicago, Phoenix, Las Vegas, Houston, Dallas, Atlanta, and the Inland Empire. With a weighted average lease term of 3.8 years remaining, the portfolio affords potential mark-to-market opportunities as leases rollover. This investment increases the Fund’s exposure to the high- performing IOS sector, which shares the same secular tailwinds as the broader industrial sector.

Footnotes

Institutional-quality: An institutional-grade, or institutional-quality property generally refers to a property of sufficient size and stature to merit attention from large national or international investors.1. As of December 31, 2024. FTFA is the investment manager of the Fund. Clarion Partners is the investment sub-adviser.

2. As of March 31, 2025 The Pearl at Boulder Creek is 4.5% of relative percentage of the holding of the entire portfolio (100%).

3. As of March 10, 2025, Clarion Partners Global Research

4. Characteristics and holdings weightings are based on total portfolio (unless otherwise noted) , are subject to change at any time, and are provided for informational purposes only. Not to be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell any security. There can be no assurance that any unrealized investment described herein will prove to be profitable. As of March 31, 2025. The investments above represent the below relative percent of the entire portfolio holdings (100%): National Industrial Outdoor Storage Portfolio 6.9%, Houston Airport Industrial Outdoor Storage 1.1%, The Pearl at Boulder Creek 4.5%

5. BLS, Moody’s Analytics, Clarion Partners Global Research

6. MSCI Real Capital Analytics, Clarion Partners Global Research

7. U.S. Census Bureau, Moody’s Analytics, CBRE-EA, Dodge, Clarion Partners Global Research

Note: Forecasts were provided by Moody’s Analytics as of 9/2024. Forecasts have certain inherent limitations and are based on complex calculations and formulas that contain substantial subjectivity and should not be relied upon as being indicative of future performance. Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Glossary

Gross domestic product (GDP): the total monetary or market value of all the finished goods and services produced within a country’s borders in a specific time period. As a broad measure of overall domestic production, it functions as a comprehensive scorecard of a given country’s economic health.

Submarket: A submarket is a smaller part of a larger real estate market. Submarkets are often identified as neighborhoods or suburbs within the larger metropolitan area.

*Some investment portfolios may include more than one property

Map of closed investments as of March 31, 2025. Please see important disclosures at the end of this document for further information. Asset allocations are based on the Net Asset Value of each asset as a percentage of the Net Asset Value of the portfolio. Geographic, Property Type and Property Holdings allocations are based on the Gross Real Estate Value of each asset as a percentage of the Gross Real Estate Value of the Private Real Estate sleeve.

Performance shown represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Current performance may be higher or lower than the performance shown. Investment return and principal value will fluctuate so shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. For performance data including the effects of sales charges, Class S shares reflect the deduction of a maximum front-end sales charge of 3.5%. Class T shares reflect the deduction of a maximum front-end sales charge of 3% and a dealer manager fee of 0.5%. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions at net asset value and the deduction of all Fund expenses. Total return figures are based on the NAV per share applied to shareholder subscriptions and redemptions, which may differ from the NAV per share disclosed in Fund shareholder reports. Performance shown excluding sales charges would have been lower, if the applicable sales charge been reflected. Had fees not been waived in various periods performance would have been lower. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For the most recent month-end information, please visit www.cprex.com.

This is not an offer to sell, or a solicitation of an offer to buy, securities. Investment in real estate and real estate derivatives entails significant risk and is suitable only for certain qualified investors as part of an overall diversified investment strategy and only for investors able to withstand a total loss of investment.

This presentation is strictly confidential and is not intended for distribution without the written permission of Clarion Partners. Any information, statement or opinion set forth herein is general in nature, is not directed to or based on the financial situation or needs of any particular investor, and does not constitute, and should not be construed as, investment advice, forecast of future events, a guarantee of future results, or a recommendation with respect to any particular security or investment strategy or type of retirement account. Investors seeking financial advice regarding the appropriateness of investing in any securities or investment strategies should consult their financial professional.

Franklin Templeton does not provide recommendations or asset allocation advice.

INVESTMENT RISKS

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All investments involve risk, including loss of principal. Diversification does not ensure against loss. An investment should be considered long-term within a multi-asset portfolio and should not be viewed individually as a complete investment program. The Fund is subject to a high degree of risk; additional risk considerations are listed:

LEVERAGE RISK:

The Fund and/or its subsidiaries employ leverage, which increases the volatility of investment returns and subjects the Fund to magnified losses if an underlying fund’s investments decline in value.

LIQUIDITY RISKS

The Fund should be viewed as a long-term investment, as it is inherently illiquid and suitable only for investors who can bear the risks associated with the limited liquidity of the Fund. Limited liquidity is provided to shareholders only through the Fund’s quarterly repurchase offers for no more than 5% of the Fund’s shares outstanding at net asset value. There is no guarantee these repurchases will occur as scheduled, or at all. Shareholders may not be able to sell their shares in the Fund at all or at a favorable price.

REAL ESTATE INVESTMENT RISKS

The Fund’s investments are highly concentrated in real estate investments, and therefore will be subject to the risks typically associated with real estate, including but not limited to fluctuations in lease occupancy rates and operating expenses, variations in rental schedules, which in turn may be adversely affected by local, state, national or international economic conditions. Such conditions may be impacted by the supply and demand for real estate properties, zoning laws, rent control laws, real property taxes, the availability and costs of financing, and environmental laws.

Furthermore, investments in real estate are also impacted by market disruptions caused by regional concerns, political upheaval, sovereign debt crises, and uninsured losses (generally from catastrophic events such as earthquakes, floods and wars). Investments in real estate related securities, such as asset-backed or mortgage-backed securities are subject to prepayment and extension risks.

PRIVATE MARKET INVESTMENTS RISKS

An investment in the Fund is suitable only for investors who can bear the risks associated with private market investments (such as private credit and private equity) with potential limited liquidity. Shares will not be listed on a public exchange, and no secondary market is expected to develop.

Any reference to or use of the terms “registered” or “registered investment adviser” does not imply that Clarion Partners or any person associated with Clarion Partners has achieved a certain level of skill or training.

Before investing, carefully consider a fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. You can find this and other information in each prospectus, and summary prospectus, if available, at www.cprex.com. Please read the prospectus carefully before investing.

Investment Approach

  • Fidelity Freedom® Funds (the Funds) are designed so that the target date referenced in the Fund name is the approximate year when investors expect to retire.
  • Except for Fidelity Freedom® Income Fund, each of the Funds seeks high total return until reaching its respective target retirement date; thereafter, each Fund's objective will be to seek high current income and, as a secondary objective, capital appreciation.
  • Except for Fidelity Freedom® Income Fund, each Fund's asset allocation strategy becomes increasingly diversified as it approaches its target date – and beyond. Ultimately, the Funds are expected to merge with Fidelity Freedom Income Fund.
  • The Funds employ a disciplined and time-tested investment process focused on helping investors achieve successful retirement outcomes by leveraging the depth and strength of Fidelity's investment research and resources.
